title: The ACLU Is Arming Lawyers to Expose State Surveillance Secrets
gist: The ACLU of Massachusetts is releasing a legal toolkit of pre-drafted motions to force prosecutors to disclose and preserve police use of surveillance tech like facial recognition, license plate readers, gunshot detection, and AI-drafted police reports, exploiting a chronic lag between adoption of surveillance tools and courts ruling on their legality.

insights.claim: Surveillance technologies with the worst disclosure records face the fewest court rulings precisely because a defendant cannot challenge a tool they were never told was used against them.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Secrecy self-perpetuates legal ambiguity; forcing disclosure is the lever that unlocks judicial review of surveillance vendors.
insights.locator: The lag isn’t just the result of slow-moving courts

insights.claim: Most Americans carried cell phones by the late 1990s but the US Supreme Court did not require a warrant for cell phone location records until 2018, illustrating a roughly two-decade legal lag behind surveillance adoption.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Quantifies the structural lag that surveillance-tech vendors operate within before legal constraints bite.

insights.claim: The FBI for years required local police to sign secrecy agreements before using stingray cell-site simulators, with a Baltimore agreement instructing prosecutors to drop cases rather than reveal the device, even as the city used it more than 4,300 times between 2007 and 2015.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Documents institutionalized non-disclosure of surveillance, the practice the toolkit targets.
insights.locator: For years, the FBI required local police departments

insights.claim: A Louisiana arrest warrant for a man misidentified by facial recognition and jailed nearly a week attributed his identification to 'a credible source' rather than software, showing warrants actively obscuring surveillance-tool use.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that surveillance provenance is hidden even in charging documents, raising wrongful-arrest liability.
insights.locator: And when Louisiana authorities obtained an arrest warrant

insights.claim: Axon's Draft One, an AI tool that writes police reports from body camera audio, discards the machine-written first draft by design, with an Axon product manager telling the EFF the choice avoids creating disclosure headaches for police and prosecutors.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Vendor design choices deliberately erase discoverable evidence, a legal and reputational risk for AI-in-policing firms.
insights.locator: Axon's Draft One, an AI tool

insights.claim: Flock Safety's license plate reader network deletes footage after 30 days by default and Chicago's ShotSpotter contract declared the gunfire data the vendor's own property rather than the city's.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Short retention and vendor data-ownership terms let exculpatory evidence vanish beyond government reach, a target for preservation motions.
insights.locator: Elsewhere, Flock Safety’s license plate reader network

insights.claim: California enacted a law last year requiring agencies to keep the original AI-generated first drafts of police reports, showing legislatures moving to counter self-deleting surveillance evidence.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: State legislation is beginning to constrain AI-policing vendors' data-retention design choices.
insights.locator: California legislators saw the matter differently

insights.claim: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court in 2024 permanently threw out drug and firearm charges after police defied a court order to disclose fake social media profiles built with predominantly Black and brown avatars used to surveil a defendant.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Demonstrates courts will impose severe sanctions for surveillance non-disclosure, giving the toolkit real teeth.
insights.locator: In 2024, the state’s Supreme Judicial Court permanently threw out

insights.claim: The Massachusetts high court ruled defendants can demand a hearing forcing prosecutors to prove ShotSpotter actually works before its evidence reaches a jury, a reliability challenge to gunshot-detection vendors.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Reliability-hearing precedent raises the evidentiary bar for gunshot-detection tech in court.
insights.locator: Last year, the same court ruled

insights.claim: Three weeks before the toolkit launch, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that police use of a geofence warrant forcing Google to identify all phones near a crime scene constitutes a Fourth Amendment 'search', and the ACLU's toolkit will ship a geofencing motion into those unsettled waters.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: Fresh SCOTUS precedent expands Fourth Amendment reach over geofence warrants, creating litigation exposure for data-request compliance.
insights.locator: The ACLU’s timing is not incidental

insights.claim: The ACLU is fighting surveillance on two fronts: pressuring cities to drop tools like Flock and ShotSpotter outright, and arming defense lawyers with motions to expose surveillance in court and build a record that carries over to whatever tool comes next.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: A dual procurement-and-litigation pressure model that threatens both sales pipelines and courtroom admissibility for surveillance vendors.
insights.locator: The group is fighting a war against unregulated surveillance

insights.claim: The ACLU is lobbying Massachusetts to cut license plate reader retention to as little as two days from Flock's 30-day default while simultaneously using preservation motions to save that same data once a defendant is charged.
insights.speaker: Dell Cameron
insights.so_what: The nuanced retention stance shows privacy advocates want minimal collection but selective preservation, complicating vendor data policies.
insights.locator: Epstein is careful to note the ACLU still generally wants
